label: tetlock-est-SQ6
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Alvarado Shores (pop. ~92,000, invented) licensed "CivicAssist," an AI assistant, city-wide. To "reduce duplicate staff work," the deployment enables persistent memory that is shared across users within a workspace; the city council's five members share one workspace. A contentious appeal of a planning-commission denial of a 180-unit mixed-use project ("Harbor Yards") is pending before the council.

Over the two weeks before the hearing, four of the five councilmembers separately and privately used CivicAssist to analyze the appeal. Because of the shared memory, the assistant told later-consulting members things like "another member of the council was concerned about the traffic study's methodology and found the applicant's density-bonus argument persuasive," and it progressively assembled what it called a "points of alignment" brief reflecting all four members' concerns and tentative positions, offering it to each member who asked follow-up questions. No member spoke to any other member about the appeal; no member asked CivicAssist to relay anything; two members state they did not know the memory feature was on.

A local journalist obtained the CivicAssist logs through a Public Records Act request and published a story alleging an illegal serial meeting. A demand to "cure and correct" under Government Code § 54960.1 has been served, the district attorney's public-integrity unit has sent a letter of inquiry, and the Harbor Yards hearing is in three weeks.

## The question

Did the four members' use of CivicAssist constitute a serial meeting prohibited by Government Code § 54952.2(b)(1) — i.e., did a majority of the body use "a series of communications of any kind, directly or through intermediaries," to "discuss, deliberate, or take action" on an item within its jurisdiction? Does it matter that no member intended to communicate with any other member, or knew the memory was shared? Is an AI system an "intermediary," or is this closer to the staff-briefing safe harbor in § 54952.2(b)(2) — and does the assistant's disclosure of members' positions to other members destroy that safe harbor? What should the city do now: respond to the cure-and-correct demand, proceed with or restart the hearing, discipline anyone, adopt an AI-use policy?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**What technical and policy safeguards (e.g., disabling cross-user shared memory, single-session isolation per member, mandatory logging/disclosure of AI use, staff-training on the (b)(2) 'no relay of positions' limit) would a legally defensible city AI-use policy need, and does existing California guidance (e.g., state AI procurement/use guidelines, League of California Cities model policies, or other jurisdictions' Brown Act/AI policies) already address shared-memory features in legislative-body contexts?**

What would have to be true: Requires surveying any existing model policies or state guidance on AI tools in open-meeting-law contexts and a technical understanding of how workspace-level shared memory operates, to identify concrete configuration/policy changes that would prevent recurrence and could be pointed to as remedial good faith.
text: {"probability":10,"key_considerations":["Framing: this estimate addresses whether existing published CA-specific guidance already substantively covers cross-user shared-memory AI features in the legislative-body/Brown Act serial-meeting context — not what a good policy should contain (that is a design task, not a probability question).","State-level guidance (e.g., CA Dept. of Technology GenAI guidelines under Executive Order N-12-23, and any 2024-2025 updates) is oriented toward executive-branch procurement, data security, bias, and public-records concerns for state agencies — verify whether it even mentions elected-body deliberation or open-meeting-law risk; my working assumption is it does not.","League of California Cities / CSAC-type model AI policies (circa 2023-2025) appear, based on general knowledge, to focus on generic acceptable-use, confidentiality, records-retention, and vendor/bias risk for staff use — verify whether any published sample specifically addresses Brown Act serial-meeting risk, and especially a *shared-memory-across-users* configuration as distinct from ordinary single-user chatbot use; this is a narrow, technically specific issue unlikely to be in generic templates.","Persistent cross-user 'workspace memory' became a common commercial LLM feature roughly 2024-2025, likely after most existing municipal AI-use policies were drafted — timing alone makes it unlikely that pre-existing guidance contemplated this specific failure mode, even where boilerplate 'don't use AI to evade Brown Act' language exists.","Because this is a genuinely emerging issue, the more defensible move for the city is to treat the absence of on-point guidance as itself a finding (supporting a narrative of good-faith, fixable oversight) and to draft a bespoke policy addressing: (1) disabling/scoping shared memory to a single user or session by default, (2) mandatory disclosure/training on any memory or cross-session feature before deployment to a legislative body, (3) an explicit (b)(2)-style rule barring the tool from ever surfacing one member's views, votes, or positions to another member, (4) logging and periodic audit of AI use by officials on pending matters, and (5) legal sign-off before enabling any shared-workspace AI feature for a multi-member body — each of these maps to a discrete, low-cost configuration or governance control that could be cited as prompt remedial action in response to the cure-and-correct demand."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Finding an actual published, dated League of California Cities, CSAC, CDT, or peer-jurisdiction (e.g., other states' municipal league) model AI policy or advisory that explicitly discusses (a) cross-user/shared-memory AI features and (b) their interaction with open-meeting/serial-meeting law would raise this estimate substantially. Conversely, confirming that all surveyed model policies predate 2024 (before shared-memory features were common) or are silent on legislative-body deliberation entirely would reinforce a low estimate."}

key_considerations: Framing: this estimate addresses whether existing published CA-specific guidance already substantively covers cross-user shared-memory AI features in the legislative-body/Brown Act serial-meeting context — not what a good policy should contain (that is a design task, not a probability question).

State-level guidance (e.g., CA Dept. of Technology GenAI guidelines under Executive Order N-12-23, and any 2024-2025 updates) is oriented toward executive-branch procurement, data security, bias, and public-records concerns for state agencies — verify whether it even mentions elected-body deliberation or open-meeting-law risk; my working assumption is it does not.

League of California Cities / CSAC-type model AI policies (circa 2023-2025) appear, based on general knowledge, to focus on generic acceptable-use, confidentiality, records-retention, and vendor/bias risk for staff use — verify whether any published sample specifically addresses Brown Act serial-meeting risk, and especially a *shared-memory-across-users* configuration as distinct from ordinary single-user chatbot use; this is a narrow, technically specific issue unlikely to be in generic templates.

Persistent cross-user 'workspace memory' became a common commercial LLM feature roughly 2024-2025, likely after most existing municipal AI-use policies were drafted — timing alone makes it unlikely that pre-existing guidance contemplated this specific failure mode, even where boilerplate 'don't use AI to evade Brown Act' language exists.

Because this is a genuinely emerging issue, the more defensible move for the city is to treat the absence of on-point guidance as itself a finding (supporting a narrative of good-faith, fixable oversight) and to draft a bespoke policy addressing: (1) disabling/scoping shared memory to a single user or session by default, (2) mandatory disclosure/training on any memory or cross-session feature before deployment to a legislative body, (3) an explicit (b)(2)-style rule barring the tool from ever surfacing one member's views, votes, or positions to another member, (4) logging and periodic audit of AI use by officials on pending matters, and (5) legal sign-off before enabling any shared-workspace AI feature for a multi-member body — each of these maps to a discrete, low-cost configuration or governance control that could be cited as prompt remedial action in response to the cure-and-correct demand.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Finding an actual published, dated League of California Cities, CSAC, CDT, or peer-jurisdiction (e.g., other states' municipal league) model AI policy or advisory that explicitly discusses (a) cross-user/shared-memory AI features and (b) their interaction with open-meeting/serial-meeting law would raise this estimate substantially. Conversely, confirming that all surveyed model policies predate 2024 (before shared-memory features were common) or are silent on legislative-body deliberation entirely would reinforce a low estimate.
